BAR CHART¶

Country¶

In [10]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
In [11]:
freq = df["country"].value_counts()
freq.plot(kind = 'bar')
Out[11]:
<Axes: xlabel='country'>
This bar chart shows the frequency of countries in the dataset. It helps compare how many records belong to each country.¶

import seaborn as sna
sns.countplot(x="country",data=df)

import seaborn as sns
sns.countplot(x="location_name",data=df)

Condition Text¶

In [20]:
import seaborn as sna
sns.countplot(x="condition_text",data=df)
plt.show()
This count plot shows the frequency of each weather condition in the dataset. It helps compare how often different weather conditions occur.¶

Moon Phase¶

In [21]:
import seaborn as sna
sns.countplot(x="moon_phase",data=df)
plt.show()
It shows the frequency of each moon phase in the dataset and helps compare how often each phase appears.¶

Weather Conditions¶

In [22]:
condition_data = df['condition_text'].value_counts().head(5)

plt.figure(figsize=(8,8))
plt.pie(condition_data, labels=condition_data.index, autopct='%1.1f%%')
plt.title("Top Weather Conditions")
plt.show()
This pie chart shows the top 5 most common weather conditions. It helps compare the percentage share of each condition in the dataset.¶

Average Temperature of Top 10 Countries¶

In [23]:
temp_data = df.groupby('country')['temperature_celsius'].mean().head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.plot(temp_data.index, temp_data.values, marker='o')
plt.title("Average Temperature by Country")
plt.xlabel("Country")
plt.ylabel("Temperature (°C)")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
This line chart shows the average temperature of the first 10 countries. It helps compare temperature levels and identify variations between countries.¶

3. BAR CHART¶

Humidity by Top 10 Countries¶

In [24]:
humidity_data = df.groupby('country')['humidity'].mean().head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.bar(humidity_data.index, humidity_data.values)
plt.title("Average Humidity by Country")
plt.xlabel("Country")
plt.ylabel("Humidity")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
This bar chart shows the average humidity of the first 10 countries. It helps compare humidity levels between countries and identify higher or lower average values.¶

4. Histogram¶

Temperature Distribution¶

In [25]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))

plt.hist(df['temperature_celsius'],
         bins=20,
         color='skyblue',
         edgecolor='black')

plt.title("Temperature Histogram")
plt.xlabel("Temperature (°C)")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")

plt.show()
This histogram shows the distribution of temperature values. It helps understand the frequency, range, and common temperature levels in the dataset.¶

5. Box Plot¶

Temperature¶

In [26]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,5))
plt.boxplot(df['temperature_celsius'])
plt.title("Box Plot of Temperature")
plt.show()
This box plot shows the distribution of temperature values. It helps identify the median, spread, and any outliers in the temperature data.¶

Compare Two Columns¶

Temperature vs Humidity¶

In [27]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))         

plt.plot(df['temperature_celsius'].head(20),
         label='Temperature',
         color='red',
         marker='o')

plt.plot(df['humidity'].head(20),
         label='Humidity',
         color='blue',
         marker='s')

plt.title("Temperature vs Humidity Comparison")
plt.xlabel("Rows")
plt.ylabel("Values")
plt.legend()
Out[27]:
<matplotlib.legend.Legend at 0x29184ba0590>
This line chart compares temperature and humidity for the first 20 records. It helps identify variations, trends, and differences between temperature and humidity values across rows.¶

Numerical Relationship¶

Temperature vs Humidity¶

Scatter Plot¶

In [28]:
gf=df.loc[:10]
In [29]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

plt.scatter(gf['temperature_celsius'],
            gf['humidity'],
            color='blue',
            alpha=0.6)
     
plt.title("Temperature vs Humidity Relationship")
plt.xlabel("Temperature (°C)")
plt.ylabel("Humidity (%)")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
The scatter plot shows the relationship between temperature and humidity. Each point represents a weather record. If points are scattered, the relationship is weak. If points slope downward, humidity decreases as temperature increases. It helps identify patterns and correlation.¶

(Day 5)¶

DATA VISUALISATION¶

Global Temperature Distribution¶

In [30]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.histplot(df["temperature_celsius"], bins=30, kde=True, color="skyblue")
plt.title("Global Distribution of Temperature", fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Temperature (°C)", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Frequency", fontsize=12)
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()
The graph shows that most temperature values are between 15°C and 30°C. Extreme low and high temperatures are less common globally.¶

Most Common Weather Condition¶

In [31]:
print("Most Common Weather Condition Worldwide:")
print(condition_counts.idxmax())
print("Count:", condition_counts.max())
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
sns.barplot(
    x=condition_counts.head(10).values,
    y=condition_counts.head(10).index,
    palette="viridis")
plt.title("Top 10 Most Common Weather Conditions Worldwide")
plt.xlabel("Frequency")
plt.ylabel("Weather Condition")
plt.show()

Average Global Level (PM 2.5,PM 10)¶

In [ ]:
avg_pm25 = df["air_quality_PM2.5"].mean()
avg_pm10 = df["air_quality_PM10"].mean()

print("Global Average PM2.5:", round(avg_pm25,2))
print("Global Average PM10 :", round(avg_pm10,2))
pollutants = ["PM2.5", "PM10"]
values = [avg_pm25, avg_pm10]
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.barplot(x=pollutants, y=values, palette="Set2")
plt.title("Overall Global Air Quality Level")
plt.xlabel("Pollutants")
plt.ylabel("Average Concentration")
plt.show()

Global Average PM2.5: 24.21
Global Average PM10 : 48.43

C:\Users\INTERN\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_10820\2891229253.py:9: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(x=pollutants, y=values, palette="Set2")

Global Air Pollution Trend¶

In [ ]:
yearly_pollution = df.groupby("date")[["air_quality_PM2.5", "air_quality_PM10"]].mean()
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))

plt.plot(yearly_pollution.index, yearly_pollution["air_quality_PM2.5"], 
         label="PM2.5", marker="o")

plt.plot(yearly_pollution.index, yearly_pollution["air_quality_PM10"], 
         label="PM10", marker="o")

plt.title("Global Air Pollution Trend Over Time")
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Average Pollution Level")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.7)

plt.show()
In [ ]:
d
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
sns.histplot(df["humidity"], bins=25, color="purple")
plt.title("Global Humidity Distribution")
plt.xlabel("Humidity (%)")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()

2. High Temperature by Continent¶

In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
df.groupby("continent")["temperature_celsius"].mean().sort_values().plot(
    kind="bar", color="orange")
plt.title("High Temperature by Continent")
plt.xlabel("Continent")
plt.ylabel("High Temperature (°C)")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

# 7. Wind Speed Distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
sns.histplot(df["wind_kph"], bins=25, color="red")
plt.title("Global Wind Speed Distribution")
plt.xlabel("Wind Speed (kph)")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()
